utterances: Can you see me?
You look nothing like you did.
It goes to the mother.
Is that part of the difficulty of looking at the situation like this where you can't separate the fate of the child from the fate of the parent?
Seeing Billy McCune was an absolutely amazing experience.
Billy defied everybody.
He did these drawings that people will probably fight over someday - and he ended up in a book - there's a portrait of him in the same book that has Braque and Picasso and Jackson Pollock in it.
(Soundbite of archived interview)
There is a book I brought.
They're pictures of artists.
This is Picasso.
Picasso - yeah, I've heard of him.
And this is Jackson Pollock.
Yeah.
Billy, they put your picture in this book.
Well, which one?
I'll show you.
That's your picture.
Yeah.
I can see the bars and uh…
So they put you in here with all these other…
I did the best I could with what I had to do with, you know.
They say Billy McCune born 1928.
And then they said that we met in 1968.
Danny Lyon met Billy McCune - an inmate serving a life sentence.
Lyon and McCune formed a bond that led to a unique artistic collaboration.
You think that's right?
I pick up a little here and there.
And so…
We spent a day driving around, and at the end of the day, he got out.

He had this used corduroy jacket he had put on and he walked by himself over the door and he opened it and I realized, I'd only seen this guy in prison and there he was going into his own house, with his own key, and he was free.
(Soundbite of archived interview)
(Unintelligible).
Billy.
And…
When we first met, we made a tape recording together.
Oh…
Correct?
Yeah.
That's how you got to me in prison and come up with ""My Last Mile.
Can you remember those lyrics?
I sure do.
Well why don't we hear those?
The jury found me guilty, they say I must pay…
(Singing) The jury found me guilty, they say I must pay.
But I have repented for both they and I.
I have asked for forgiveness.
I prayed and I cried.
I want the Lord with me as I walk my last mile.
(Soundbite of music)
Billy McCune died Monday in Kansas City.
He was 79 years old.
Documentary photographer Danny Lyon lives in Upstate New York.
Our story was produced by Matt Ozug, with help from Dave Isay at Sound Portraits Productions in New York City.
Instrumental music by Nick Yulman and Lisa Wong(ph).
You can find a link to Billy McCune's prison artwork at npr.org.
In a few minutes, the most anticipated event of the 2006 Winter Olympics gets underway: the women's figure skating long program, also known as the free skate.
A slim 3/100 of a point separate leaders Sasha Cohen and Irina Slutskaya, and two Japanese skaters are not far behind.
As we've seen with other sports in this Olympics, anything can happen and anyone could still win the gold.
For more we go now to Turin where Geoff Calkins, the sport columnist for the Memphis Commercial Appeal is covering the event.
Geoff, nice to have you back on the program.
Geoff Calkins, are you there?
We're having some technical difficulties.
And as I understand it, the women's long program, the free skate, is scheduled to get under way just at the top of the hour coming up; four o'clock eastern time.
And maybe sometime before those of us who watch it on TV here in the States get an opportunity to see what Sasha Cohen and Irina Slutskaya actually look like when they do their programs.
Also the fine Japanese skaters.
There's two more Americans in the top seven.
Geoff Calkins, are you there?
I am here, absolutely.
We are all a-tingle as we are waiting for, roughly twenty minutes, for the final group of six to come out and see who will be crowned in this most-glamorous of all Olympic events.
Any last-minute drama there today?

Well, the only drama was, Sasha Cohen, of course, did not practice yesterday, which caused quite an amount of twittering among people who are serious about covering figure skating, that she skipped her practice.
And it's sort of interesting that the music is all, you know, sort of lined up in advance.
Even though she didn't skate, you still have the theme from Romeo and Juliet playing as no one's out there.
It's sort of draws attention to the fact that she's not practicing.
It's the equivalent of building a sandcastle, and you kind of know what happens.
You know, after a while, the ocean will do what the ocean's going to do.
It depends how hard you want to fight it I guess.
Well, thank you.
Kaleen Cottingham is the director of Washington state's Recreation and Conservation Office.
She says the NookSack Tribe can't legally sign off on $6 million from her office.
That's a part of the $14 million the tribe says it's been denied from the state and federal government.
It's now suing the feds because of it.
